Q1: My study routine was honestly a bit scattered because I'm balancing a lot at 27 with work and other responsibilities. I tried to carve out time in the evenings after my day job, usually around 13 hours a week total. I'm an auditory learner, so I'd often listen to lecture recordings while commuting or doing chores. It wasn't the most structured approach, but I made it work with what I had.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be consistent with my evening study sessions, but other weeks deadlines would pile up and I'd have to cram. Being older and having more responsibilities means I can't always stick to a rigid schedule, so I learned to be flexible and adapt as needed.
Q3: Usually it was checking for new announcements or seeing if grades were posted that got me to log in. I'd also log in when I knew a deadline was coming up or when I wanted to catch up on discussion posts. Sometimes I'd get a notification on my phone and that would prompt me to check in, even if briefly.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks I got into a rhythm where I'd check the LMS first thing in the morning with my coffee and then again in the evening. It became almost automatic, like checking email. The peer support group I was connected with helped because we'd remind each other about things posted on the platform, which reinforced the habit.
Q5: The video lectures were the most valuable for me because I could listen to them multiple times and really absorb the information through hearing it. I watched about 84 minutes worth on average, which helped compensate for classes I couldn't attend in person. The discussion forums were also useful when peers explained concepts in different ways than the instructor.
Q6: I'll be honest, I often skipped the reading materials and some of the supplementary PDFs. I found it harder to engage with text-heavy content compared to audio or video. Some of the optional quizzes I also delayed because they felt less urgent when I had assignments piling up.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could immediately see how it connected to an assignment or exam. If I could listen to it rather than read it, even better. I also valued activities where I could interact with peers because those conversations often clarified things faster than studying alone.
Q8: Optional activities definitely felt different because there was no immediate consequence for skipping them. Required work had a deadline attached, so there was urgency. With optional stuff, I'd tell myself I'd get to it later, but later rarely came because something required would always take priority.
Q9: Absolutely. I downloaded a lot of the audio lectures and listened to them on my phone during my commute and at the gym. I also took handwritten notes during these sessions that never showed up in any system. Some weeks, most of my actual learning happened completely offline.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my notes or listening to downloaded lectures for hours, but the LMS wouldn't capture any of that. I'd also call up a classmate from my peer support group and we'd talk through problems together on the phone. That was real studying, but invisible to the system.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd download a lecture video or audio file once, then replay it several times over the next few days while doing other things. The LMS would only show that single access, but I might have listened to that same content three or four times offline.
Q12: Around midterms, things got really hard. My work schedule picked up and I was exhausted. My attendance dropped and I fell behind on assignments. I felt surprised by how quickly everything piled up. The peer support connection helped me push through, but there were definitely low points.
Q13: Work responsibilities were the biggest factor affecting my LMS use. When I was tired after a long day, I'd log in but not really absorb much. Confusion about certain topics also made me avoid those modules temporarily. I had to prioritize ruthlessly, which meant some things got neglected.
Q14: There were times I'd scroll through materials or have a video playing but my mind was elsewhere. I was technically logged in and active, but I wasn't really learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine engagement.
Q15: I postponed assignments that seemed overwhelming or unclear. Even knowing they were important, I'd push them off hoping I'd feel more capable later. Sometimes it was pure exhaustion, other times it was anxiety about not doing well. I usually came back to them eventually, but not always with enough time.
Q16: Deadlines were my main driver for LMS activity. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly log in more frequently, watch videos faster, and post in discussions. Without deadlines, I probably would have engaged much less. They created the urgency I needed to act.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more with similar activities. Critical feedback sometimes made me hesitant to try again right away. The peer support group was helpful because they'd encourage me to see feedback as constructive rather than discouraging.
Q18: The course structure with weekly modules helped me pace myself somewhat, even if I didn't always keep up perfectly. Having clear expectations for each week gave me targets to aim for. When the structure was loose, I tended to drift and fall behind more easily.
Q19: Having audio and video options made engagement much easier for me as an auditory learner. What made it harder was when important information was buried in long text documents. Also, when assignment instructions were unclear, I'd avoid starting them until I absolutely had to.
Q20: I think students might be searching for information, trying to understand requirements, or just feeling anxious and checking things repeatedly without actually completing work. Sometimes high activity is a sign of confusion or struggling to figure out what's expected rather than productive engagement.
Q21: That definitely reflects my experience somewhat. I did a lot of learning offline through downloaded materials and peer discussions that wouldn't show in the logs. Someone could look at my moderate LMS activity and not realize how much time I spent studying away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when you're most active, which resources you access, and whether you meet deadlines. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, and the emotional experience of learning. They can't capture a phone conversation with a peer that clarified everything.
Q23: Being engaged means genuinely trying to understand and apply the material, not just completing tasks. It's about feeling connected to the content and the learning community. For me, engagement includes those moments of real understanding, whether they happen online or offline.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show my resilience in pushing through difficult weeks, the support I got from peers outside the platform, or the offline listening sessions that were actually my primary learning method. It also wouldn't capture how surprised I was by my own ability to keep going despite challenges.
